title: The Incarnation
summary: The eternal Son of God took on human flesh — not by ceasing to be God, but by adding humanity to himself — entering history as the only mediator between a holy God and fallen humanity.
scripture: John 1:14 — "And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we have seen his glory, glory as of the only Son from the Father, full of grace and truth."
detail: The Incarnation is not a mythological accommodation — it is the necessary theological precondition for atonement. The Council of Chalcedon (451 AD) formalized what the New Testament had already established: Christ possesses two complete natures, divine and human, united in one person without confusion, change, division, or separation. This precision matters. Only one truly human could legally represent humanity before God's law. Only one truly divine could bear infinite penalty and satisfy infinite justice. The doctrine is not a late Hellenistic interpolation — it appears in Paul's Philippian letter (2:6–11), written within two decades of the crucifixion, and in the Johannine prologue, which explicitly identifies the pre-existent Word as the agent of creation who then became flesh. The Incarnation is the hinge of history because it is the only event in which the divine and human are personally united — not merely in communication, but in person.
apologeticEdge: Islam rejects the Incarnation on the grounds of tawhid — divine unity — arguing that God cannot take on human form without compromising his transcendence. But this objection assumes a unitarian conception of God that is itself philosophically thin. If God is eternally relational within his own being (Father, Son, Spirit), then entering into relationship with creation through the Son is not a contradiction — it is a consistent extension of his nature. The Islamic alternative, that Jesus was a prophet only, leaves unanswered how the Quran itself calls him the Word of God (kalimat Allah, Surah 4:171) and Spirit from God — categories applied to no other prophet. The Islamic Jesus is over-qualified for prophethood and under-qualified for the role Islam assigns him.

title: The Resurrection
summary: Jesus Christ rose bodily from the dead on the third day — a historical event, not a spiritual metaphor — vindicating his claims, defeating death, and inaugurating the new creation.
scripture: 1 Corinthians 15:17 — "And if Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile and you are still in your sins."
detail: Paul's statement is the most intellectually honest claim in all of religious literature: Christianity rises or falls on a verifiable historical event. The minimal facts accepted even by hostile critical scholars are these — Jesus died by Roman crucifixion; he was buried; the tomb was found empty by women on the third day; post-resurrection appearances were reported to individuals and groups, including over five hundred simultaneously (1 Cor. 15:6); and the disciples underwent a radical, inexplicable transformation from terrified fugitives to martyrs unwilling to recant. No alternative hypothesis — theft, hallucination, swoon, legend — accounts for all five data points simultaneously. The hallucination hypothesis, for instance, cannot explain the empty tomb. The theft hypothesis cannot explain the disciples' willingness to die for what they would have known to be a fabrication. N.T. Wright's exhaustive historical analysis concludes that bodily resurrection is the only explanation that fits the evidence within its first-century Jewish context.
apologeticEdge: Islam's response to the Resurrection is the substitution theory — that God replaced Jesus on the cross with someone else (commonly cited as Simon of Cyrene or Judas), making it appear he was crucified (Surah 4:157). This is a remarkable position. It requires God to have deceived millions of witnesses, created a religion (Christianity) built on a divine illusion, and allowed billions of people to worship Jesus as risen Lord based on a cosmic misdirection. This makes God the author of the greatest deception in human history. The substitution theory also has zero early historical attestation — it appears in Gnostic texts centuries later and was never the position of those who actually witnessed the events. The Quran's denial of the crucifixion contradicts every independent historical source — Roman, Jewish, and Christian — making it the weakest historical claim Islam makes about Jesus.

title: The Trinity
summary: The one God eternally exists as three distinct persons — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — co-equal, co-eternal, and of one essence. This is not a late development. It is the only coherent account of the God revealed in Scripture.
scripture: Matthew 28:19 — "Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit."
detail: Trinitarianism must be distinguished from two errors it is routinely confused with: tritheism (three separate gods) and modalism (one God wearing three sequential masks). The Nicene formulation (325 AD, refined 381 AD) holds that God is one in essence (ousia) and three in person (hypostasis) — distinctions that are real, eternal, and not merely relational roles adopted for human benefit. Each person is fully God. There is only one God. These are not contradictory claims — they operate at different levels of predication. The Trinitarian structure is not a Greek philosophical imposition on a Hebrew original — it is embedded in the earliest strata of the New Testament. Paul's benediction in 2 Corinthians 13:14 invokes all three persons. The Johannine prologue identifies the Son as the pre-existent divine Word. Pentecost involves all three persons acting distinctly and simultaneously. The councils did not invent the Trinity — they defended the grammar needed to protect what the apostles had already written.
apologeticEdge: Islam charges that the Trinity is shirk — associating partners with God — and that Christians worship three gods. This is a misreading the Quran appears to share, since Surah 5:116 suggests the Trinity consists of God, Jesus, and Mary — a formulation no Christian tradition has ever held. If Islam's primary objection is against a doctrine Christians do not hold, the critique does not land. More fundamentally, Islam's strict unitarianism (tawhid) creates a philosophical problem it cannot resolve: if God is love (a Quranic attribute), who did he love before creation? Love requires an object. A solitary, pre-creation God who is love by nature but had nothing to love until he created is incoherent. The Trinitarian God is love in his eternal being — Father, Son, and Spirit in perfect self-giving communion — and creation is an overflow of that love, not its precondition.

title: Scripture and Revelation
summary: The Bible is the inspired, inerrant, and sufficient Word of God — the only infallible rule of faith and practice — given through human authors under the Holy Spirit's superintendence.
scripture: 2 Timothy 3:16–17 — "All Scripture is breathed out by God and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness."
detail: The Reformed doctrine of Scripture goes beyond general inspiration to affirm inerrancy (Scripture is without error in all it affirms) and sufficiency (Scripture contains everything necessary for salvation and godliness — no further revelation is required or expected). The term theopneustos — God-breathed — describes not the authors but the text itself: what was written is the product of divine breath. This does not imply mechanical dictation. The human authors wrote freely in their own styles, from their own cultural locations, under divine superintendence that ensured the result was precisely what God intended. The canon was not created by church councils but recognized — texts already bearing apostolic authority, consistent with the rule of faith, and received by the churches were acknowledged as canonical. The manuscript tradition for the New Testament is without parallel in ancient literature: over 5,800 Greek manuscripts, with a textual reliability rate exceeding 99%. The unity of the biblical narrative — creation, fall, redemption, consummation — across 66 books, 40 authors, and 1,500 years is its own argument for a single intelligence superintending the whole.
apologeticEdge: Islam charges that the Bible has been corrupted (tahrif) — altered by Jews and Christians to remove references to Muhammad and distort the original revelation. This charge faces a crippling evidential problem: it is never specified when, where, by whom, or how this corruption occurred. The Dead Sea Scrolls confirm the Hebrew Old Testament's stability back to 200 BC. New Testament manuscripts predate the rise of Islam by centuries and show no evidence of systematic theological corruption. If the Bible was corrupted before Islam, the Quran's instruction to Jews and Christians to judge by their scriptures (Surah 5:47, 5:68) becomes incoherent — God is directing people to a corrupted source. If it was corrupted after Islam, the manuscript tradition refutes it. The tahrif charge is historically unanchored and logically self-defeating.

title: Substitutionary Atonement
summary: God, being both perfectly just and perfectly merciful, provided the only sufficient payment for human sin: his own Son as substitute — bearing the penalty we deserved so that we might receive the righteousness we could never earn.
scripture: Isaiah 53:5 — "But he was pierced for our transgressions; he was crushed for our iniquities; upon him was the chastisement that brought us peace, and with his wounds we are healed."
detail: Penal substitutionary atonement is the load-bearing arch of Reformed soteriology. God's justice is not a characteristic that can be suspended — it is intrinsic to his nature. Sin against an infinitely holy God carries infinite consequences. No finite act of repentance, charity, or religious devotion can satisfy an infinite debt. The solution Scripture provides is not that God overlooks sin — it is that God himself absorbs the penalty. The Son, fully human and therefore legally qualified to represent humanity, and fully divine and therefore able to bear infinite penalty, stood in the place of sinners. The result is double imputation: our sin is credited to Christ; his righteousness is credited to us. This is not a transaction God was forced into — it is the eternal plan of a God whose love and justice are both satisfied in the cross. Romans 3:26 identifies this as the mechanism by which God is simultaneously just and the justifier of the ungodly.
apologeticEdge: Islam rejects substitutionary atonement on two grounds: first, that a just God would not punish the innocent for the guilty; second, that human beings do not require a substitute because sin is forgiven by sincere repentance alone. Both objections misread the logic of the cross. The Son is not an unwilling third party coerced into bearing punishment — he is God himself, voluntarily absorbing the consequence of his own law into his own person. This is not injustice; it is the most profound act of self-giving justice conceivable. The second objection exposes a deeper problem in Islamic theology: if God forgives sin simply by fiat, with no satisfaction of justice, then God's forgiveness is arbitrary — it reflects will, not character. The cross demonstrates that God's forgiveness is costly, morally coherent, and rooted in who he eternally is. Islam offers no account of how a holy God can be just and forgive sin simultaneously. The cross is precisely that account.

title: The Nature of God
summary: God is the self-existent, necessary, and personal being — not merely a first cause among causes, but Being itself — who reveals himself as sovereign, holy, loving, and relational.
scripture: Exodus 3:14 — "God said to Moses, 'I AM WHO I AM.' And he said, 'Say this to the people of Israel: I AM has sent me to you.'"
detail: Classical theism, running through Augustine, Anselm, and the Reformed scholastics, holds that God is not a contingent being who happens to exist — he is ipsum esse subsistens, Being itself, whose essence is existence. He cannot not exist. This distinguishes the God of Scripture from every other proposed deity and from the deist unmoved mover: he is not merely the most powerful being in a universe of beings, but the ground of all being. He is also irreducibly personal — not an impersonal force or a philosophical abstraction, but a self-revealing, speaking, covenant-making God who enters history with intention and particularity. His attributes are not a list of independent properties but a unified whole: his omniscience, omnipotence, holiness, and love are mutually entailing. A perfectly good God must be all-knowing — ignorance compromises moral agency. A truly sovereign God must be providentially active — deism's absentee God is not the God of Scripture. The divine name I AM is the most philosophically loaded declaration in ancient literature: it identifies God as the one being whose nature is to be.
apologeticEdge: Islam and Christianity share significant common ground on God's unity, eternality, and sovereignty — and this common ground should be acknowledged before pressing the distinctions. The critical divergence is whether God is personal in his eternal being or merely personal in his acts toward creation. Islamic theology, particularly in its Ash'arite dominant tradition, holds that God's attributes are so transcendent as to be essentially unknowable — his will is the final explanation for all things, including what is good and evil (voluntarism). This produces a God whose goodness is defined by whatever he wills, rather than a God who wills in accordance with his eternal goodness. The Reformed God's sovereignty is never arbitrary — it flows from perfect wisdom, holiness, and love. The I AM who reveals himself to Moses is not the God of pure will, but the God of covenant faithfulness — a distinction with enormous moral and epistemological consequences.
